description: Law governing alternative dispute resolution through arbitration and conciliation as alternatives to court litigation.

sections.title: Arbitration Agreement
sections.text: An arbitration agreement means an agreement by the parties to submit to arbitration all or certain disputes which have arisen or which may arise between them. The arbitration agreement may be in the form of an arbitration clause in a contract or in the form of a separate agreement.
sections.simplified: An arbitration agreement is when two parties agree that if a dispute arises, they will resolve it through arbitration (a private judge) instead of going to court. This can be a clause in a contract or a separate agreement.

sections.title: Appointment of Arbitrators
sections.text: A person of any nationality may be an arbitrator. The parties are free to agree on a procedure for appointing the arbitrator(s). Failing any agreement, in arbitration with three arbitrators, each party appoints one, and the two appointed arbitrators appoint the third. The High Court or Supreme Court may appoint if parties fail to agree.
sections.simplified: Arbitrators are like private judges. The parties can choose who the arbitrator is. If they can't agree, the High Court can appoint one. The arbitrator can be of any nationality.

sections.title: Challenging Arbitral Award
sections.text: An application for setting aside an arbitral award may be made to the court within 3 months from the date of receipt of the award. The court may set aside the award if: (a) party was under some incapacity, (b) arbitration agreement was not valid, (c) party was not given proper notice, (d) award deals with a dispute not covered by the agreement, (e) composition of tribunal was not in accordance with agreement.
sections.simplified: If you disagree with an arbitration award, you can challenge it in court within 3 months. But the grounds for challenge are limited — you can't just say the arbitrator was wrong; you need to show procedural irregularities.

sections.title: Enforcement of Arbitral Award
sections.text: Where the time for making an application to set aside the arbitral award has expired, or such application having been made, it has been refused, the award shall be enforced as if it were a decree of the court.
sections.simplified: Once the 3-month challenge period passes (or if your challenge is rejected), the arbitration award becomes as good as a court decree and can be enforced like one — including through execution proceedings.

sections.title: Interim Measures by Court
sections.text: A party may, before or during arbitral proceedings or at any time after making of the arbitral award but before it is enforced, apply to the court for interim measures of protection.
sections.simplified: Even in arbitration, you can approach the court for emergency interim orders (like injunctions, freezing bank accounts, or appointment of receivers) before, during, or after the arbitration.
